speaker: interviewer
text: --Wineman. We're in London, England. And this is tape 3. Somebody handed the baby to your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, as, as-- I had a coat with me. As my dress was torn, I put on the coat. It was a red coat. And I was walking with my mother and my young brother, Marcel, walking. I know now it was towards the crematorium. But at the time, I didn't know where I was walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I was already a little way up that line of people that were going, I was called back by Mengele. Was it the coat? What was it? I don't know. So he called us back. And he said to me, you know, dressed in his-- in his shiny boots and smiling, and so on and so forth. He said to me, you go this way. So I said to him, I don't want to be separated from my mother. I said, I want to remain with my-- stay with my mother. I want to go wherever my mother goes. So he said no, no, no. You go this way. He said, your mother, she's going to look after the children and the babies. She's going this way. And your mother was still carrying the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was carrying the baby. And she was walking with my brother. And my mother immediately said to me, that is the end. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was the last time I saw my mother. And when I walked to-- [CRYING] looked at the other side, I saw my father and my two brothers. And my father, this was the last time I saw him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, of course, I found out afterwards that the young woman behind us-- who was a pretty, young woman from Holland, was 21, she was-- she had borne the child in the prison already in Holland. And having given the child to my mother, she was-- she wanted to know what was happening to her child. The child was six weeks old, the baby. I don't even know if it was a girl or a boy. And she kept asking me, because she was in the same barrack as I was, our address, where we would find-- where she would be-- find us after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she kept-- every time she saw me-- she was not always in the same Kommando-- she, she repeated the address to say, is it correct? Is it right? Have I forgotten anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what I can say? She, she survived Auschwitz. She survived Bergen-Belsen. But she died in the third camp. She did.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was-- I was taken with the other 40 or 50 girls to the side. And Janine was with me. And Janine was a family of four, that's it. She had a sister called [? Huguette. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Dreyfus family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Dreyfus family. So the two girls were also-- got in. Their parents didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were also younger girls. They were 18 and 17. So we, we were taken to, to a room. Well, first, we were taken to a place where they asked us to leave our clothes. And she started to tattoo us, the girl. It was a Jewish girl, a young Jewish girl. So she, she probably had pity on us. She gave us small numbers, instead of making big numbers. And then we were taken. She took off the little ring I had, a watch. And they took whatever we had on us. And we were taken to what looked like a shower room. But it was-- could have been gas, you know. But it happened to be water. And then we were taken to, to dry. And afterwards, they-- we asked already questions there. There was a woman called Marla, who later is, is known to have tried to escape. She spoke, they say, 16 languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were there, sitting there, asking questions, asking what happened to the people that didn't come in. What happened? What is happening? What is it all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She tried very hard to get us to understand that maybe we will not see our dear ones anymore. But you don't accept that in moments like that, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next thing I knew, we were taken to another big room of some kind, hole. And there, they were shaving our hair. And a few of our French girls, real beauties, they tried not to have it shaved. Two or three escaped, thinking perhaps it would help if they have hair-- not to be shaved. Then we were-- we had boxes. You know, they had no stripe-- there were so many people arriving, they didn't have any striped clothes. They just gave you a, a, a, a, a dress from here or skirt from here, a shoe from here. So I had one shoe with a heel, one without a heel, a little dress, a green dress, I remember, with a little, white collar-- and completely dehumanized in a matter of a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went out into the open. And I could only see two ears, you know? And I did-- we-- the girls I saw before and after, I didn't recognize them. I didn't know if they-- who was it, you know-- and completely disturbed, completely dehumanized. They had taken everything away from us. In, in no time, we were not anymore the same person.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we kept on crying. It didn't help very much. And then they, they, they made us walk into a barrack, you know. I don't know how many we were-- several hundred. We get into these-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not beds, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bunks, wooden bunks, with one blanket for four or five or six, I don't remember. And we were so dejected, you know. And the next thing we knew, we, we asked questions. We tried to get an answer from the so-called other inmates, you know. I only knew that the next day, when we were on the-- on the Appell, on the call-- 'roll call,' they call it, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right away, the beatings started, you know, people who couldn't stand, who were too tired-- tired, or who were not behaving according to what the, the system there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- the only thing we were given, really, was-- in a-- in a-- a, a tin container, there was some kind of water with something in it. But, you see, it had to pass from the-- from the beginning to the end. So if you were in the middle, if-- whatever they add, you got one sip or two. And that was your lot. Whatever it was-- it was just water for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only thing I remember quite, quite clearly was some people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did get in with their mothers, you know. And when I saw-- when the mother couldn't stand the appel for so many hours, when they collapsed and the kapo went and started to beat them up, I said, would I have been able to take that, that a kapo or a guard, a German guard, should go and beat my mother up, and I can't do anything about it? How would I be able to take that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were sent right away the next day. And I think I was in a terrible state because I think I wasn't normal for a few days. It's a shock. I, I was under-- in shock. Because they were sent-- we were sent to-- near the railways to make trenches. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we saw arriving the ghetto from Litzmannstadt, the Lodz ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And these people, you could see that they had gone through hell already. They were already skin, skin and bones, badly dressed, in a terrible way. And I asked them where they were coming from. They said they hadn't noticed that we were working there. I asked them where they're coming from. And they told us they came from Litzmannstadt. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nobody came in of these people, nobody. They just passed us. Nobody was allowed into the camp, of that convoy anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went straight to, to the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crematorium. You see, what we noticed very soon, we were-- we were doing trenches and trenches and trenches. But we, we didn't know why we were doing these trenches, you know? But we soon understood because we were somehow-- our block was facing the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we saw fire-- and the smell, and the-- the terrible smell in the camp, and the flames. But also sometimes we saw flames coming from the trenches, you see, further along, in the open. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't want to believe all that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't understand what was happening. We kept asking questions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you could-- and the people that were there already a long time were very hard with us, though. They're so-- they, they made us understand that's how we will finish off, you know. They didn't mince their words. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I soon-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] on the 17th of August, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a very big selection. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And on-- I must tell you one thing. Because when I arrived and I could see that we were already, you know, dehumanized completely, I said, I must have at least a toothbrush, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first few days, I, I bartered a piece of bread for a toothbrush. So I had a toothbrush. That's about all I had there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the 17th of August-- trying to keep myself at least as clean as possible, you know, not to take my whole dignity away, not to make me completely a Muselmann. So I-- there was a big selection. Everybody was under the Appellplatz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a few-- the SS came towards us. And she said to me, you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then she said-- Janine said, I'm her sister. She looked a bit like me. Yes. Then she said, she's my sister, [? Huguette. ?] She was her sister. No-- didn't take her. Then she took the person, [? Susanne, ?] who came also from the same convoy. She took her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were about 50 girls. We didn't know for what. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was to be the Kommando Kanada. you must have heard-- the sorting Kommando, which we didn't know it was at the time. But you know what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so Janine's sister [? Huguette, ?] you see, didn't get in. So she was separated from her from then on. Once you were separated, you were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know that you were going to be-- never see [? Huguette ?] again at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw her afterwards, sometimes, after work. But she was separated from us. She was not anymore in the same Kommando. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't know what it was. So we walked out in the morning. The-- I remember the first day of that Kommando Kanada. We walked out of the Birkenau camp. We walked towards somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we walked out-- it was the first day-- a group of men were coming towards us. And in that group was my brother, David. So I knew he was alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, when we arrived in the Kommando Kanada, we could see what was happening there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were these mountains of clothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some people recognized their family, you know, the case, photos. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had to sort out these clothes in parcels of so-and-so-and-so. And they were sent to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to sort out-- separating them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had pullovers-- yes, separating them. There were, you know, the pullovers-- the pullovers, this or that, and so on and so forth, and made-- make bundles. I remember we made bundles. And and for-- because we worked in that Kommando, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Which was a terrible-- the-- what you got for it when you came home, you got an extra ration of margarine and sausage, I think, for that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- when we came back from that Kommando Kanada, we met up with the other girls from the same convoy that were sent to trenches and hard work. They were beaten up so hard that when they had to bring one-- or at least one dead person home every night. So, of course, they were in a terrible way. They were beaten up. They couldn't do the hard work. Or they did the hard work, but their clothes were torn. There were wounded-- terrible. And so we could help them a little bit by giving one of them a little bit of our ration that we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What we did next [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by working there is that we started to want to help our friends. So we smuggled things in. We would take the old shoes that were from the-- worn out from the mud and things, and come back with a good pair. Or we would come back with underwear-- it was getting cold-- and so on. But one day, our group was stopped. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the SS at the door, at the entrance-- and the SS asked three girls, [POINTS] like this, you, you, and you come forward and undress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, they found clothing on them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we had to witness the hanging of the three girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though it was-- the rest of you also had things, they only hung--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the three.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had-- and we were not anymore in that Kommando-- finished. Instead, we were sent to a punishing Kommando. We were punished. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we must have worked a few weeks there like that, doing that, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helped our friends. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you said you were in a punishment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then we were-- the punishment was we were in a Kommando to do trenches, our group, with no food [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and beaten up and the dogs behind us all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But one day, as we were coming out of this Kommando of the work, three little girls were-- three young girls were waiting there. And they said we have-- they weren't working. They were Hungarians. They had their hair, they had no number. They were meant for the crematorium. But they were-- it was a time when they were arriving all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, they could see what was happening to us. So they said, listen, we are three of us-- in Yiddish. They said, you have one piece of bread from ours. We have three. You know, each gets one piece. So we give you one, and you share it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that saved us. They did this for a week or 10 days-- they did that. Every night, they were there standing there with their piece of bread. I got to know them a bit, you know, because I remember their color hair. And what was so amazing, there was one of them who was very religious. So she managed not to work on Shabbat. She hid on Shabbat. She didn't eat the non-kosher food. And-- but I go on with this little story because it's so amazing. And when I came back, I never told my girls what's happened to me in the camp. But I always told them about these Hungarian girls who, who really saved us. And when I went to the big gathering in Israel, in Jerusalem, somebody came towards me. And she spoke American. And I spoke English. And she asked me which barracks I was. And I said I didn't remember. I was in four camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said-- I looked at her. And I said, you know, this face is familiar. There were thousands of people at that gathering. So I said, are you-- she said, I'm a-- I was Hungarian. And I said, yes, and I am French. So she said-- I said, were you one of three sisters? And I described exactly how I knew them, the color hair they had, that one was so religious, that we went to see them in their barracks and they were like beasts, you know, lying there. It was them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had survived, all three of them, on the long march from Auschwitz. And she was now married to an American captain. And the other was in Sao Paulo. And they were all there. And we have met since. But this was an unusual encounter to meet a-- especially what she did at times like that. Well, that was only the beginning of the punishing, because the punishing went on after that the whole time. And the selections went on the whole time. And the camp-- you-- we thought there was no more daylight. It was black from the smoke-- and the smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we all got [NON-ENGLISH], you know, boils. And, and I was sick. And I had frozen feet. And, and I was crying and depressed because I couldn't see any end, you know. Really and truly, you, you, you wondered, why should you suffer such a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the suffering was not only physical. The suffering was what you saw. You saw these people arrive. And you only saw smoke. My parents' best friends comes from Lyon-- arrived in August. And we heard-- you know, in June, when we arrived on the 3rd of June in 1944, or the 2nd of June in 1944, we heard that the 6th of June was invasion. And it was terrible, you know, to hear how unlucky we were, you know. But in August, they still were bringing people from France. There were already four weeks on the way. And in, in September, they brought even in still the Dutch-- you know, Anne Frank was in a convoy of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were arriving still all the time, especially the Hungarians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The ones that were before us were all-- the Czech were there next to us. We could converse with the Czech because quite a few Czech people spoke French.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were then still in all the time-- the Kartoffel-- 'potato'-- Kommando was a horrible Kommando where we had to work getting the potatoes from wagons into the silos, you know, for the winter. And we were beaten up terribly. I was beaten up with a-- the cane, it fell on my head. It made such a crack that, until recently, I thought maybe I had a crack there because it was really-- I had headaches. So after that, we didn't have any-- only, only, only, only trenches we did after that till we were on the-- on the new-- on the other selection, a big selection. Because you mustn't forget, every time when there was a selection, when you went through, Mengele was-- most of the time was there, you never knew if it was your last day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some girls, you know, tried to pinch their cheeks, you know, they should look rosy, or they shouldn't get too thin. But you couldn't help it, you know. With the diet you were on and the work you worked, you weren't supposed to live. So you, you gradually went down, down, down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There we were-- we were selected again here. And we didn't know what we were selected for. But we were selected in that convoy of a thousand girls that went to Bergen-Belsen on the 31st of October. By then, we didn't look a pretty sight. And we were put in a train. I don't know how many days we travelled. We arrived in Bergen-Belsen in a terrible state. I just spoke to [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was in the same convoy because the dates are exactly the same. When we arrived there, they put us in tents. They had no proper barracks for us. And there was a terrible storm. And the barracks collapsed-- the tents collapsed. And they fell onto us. And then we were put somewhere where we were in the open. We had nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I recently asked-- and now-- Janine, my sister-in-law, and other people, I said, I cannot remember that we got food in Bergen-Belsen. Can you remember if we got food? They said only sometimes. We didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Bergen-Belsen, actually, they put us in the tent. We were-- after, you know, we traveled in these trucks on straw, dirty, no food, no, no, no, no hygiene, no nothing-- for the flies, I suppose, already. We were lying-- we couldn't even lie like sardines. We had to lay like squashed sardines. Therefore, a girl that was sleeping like a sardine and with her legs on my tummy scratched me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that scratch became an anthrax [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because, there-- you know, there were dirty feet and dirty nails. And to this day, I feel it, because eventually, they put in-- they didn't have much, you know. It became an anthrax. And she had nothing. So without anesthetic or anything, she, she pierced it, put some kind of gauze in it with a disinfectant, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but there was nobody to look after it. Because after that-- that was-- must have been quite a time later, because when they took it out, there was now maggots in it, full of maggots, you know. It was just maggots that had grown in there. And that left me with a scar that I can even today tell the change of weather.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the conditions in Bergen-Belsen were just that you were here to die, you know? We were in barracks, except for being-- go for Appell, being beaten up for no reason whatsoever. Food I cannot remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on one Appell on the 1st of January-- because I will never forget it because I knew it was the 1st of January-- they made a general Appell-- 'Appell,' it means 'call-up' in Bergen-Belsen-- where everybody had to come out in the open and stand. The sick, the old, the-- everybody had to come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had to stand there 12 hours in the snow and ice. And they just wanted to watch how many will die. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a few of us came out of that. Why? We don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was the 1st of January, '45, yeah?
